title: STAMPEDE

trialDesign.fullName: Surgical Therapy And Medications Potentially Eradicate Diabetes Efficiently (STAMPEDE)
trialDesign.design: Randomized, nonblinded, single-center trial (3 parallel arms)
trialDesign.population: 150 obese patients (BMI 27–43) with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes; mean HbA1c 9.2%
trialDesign.sampleSize: n=150 (50 per group: medical vs gastric bypass vs sleeve gastrectomy)
trialDesign.intervention: Intensive medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy
trialDesign.comparator: Intensive medical therapy alone (ADA-guided glycemic, lipid, BP management)
trialDesign.primaryEndpoint: HbA1c ≤6.0% at 12 months (with or without diabetes medications)
trialDesign.overview: Surgery plus medical therapy achieved glycemic target in 42% (bypass) and 37% (sleeve) vs 12% with medical therapy alone.

headings.background: Observational data suggested bariatric surgery improves type 2 diabetes; few RCTs in moderately obese patients.
headings.design: 150 patients randomized to intensive medical therapy, bypass, or sleeve; insulin use stratified.
headings.population: Mean age 49; 66% women; mean diabetes duration ~8 years; 44% on insulin at baseline.
headings.intervention: Roux-en-Y bypass or sleeve gastrectomy plus intensive medical therapy.
headings.comparator: Intensive medical therapy with quarterly specialist visits and medication escalation to HbA1c ≤6.0%.
headings.followUp: 12 months; nonblinded; 10 patients excluded from 12-month analysis (withdrawals/missed visits).

safetyHighlights: No deaths or life-threatening complications; 4 reoperations
Hospitalization in 22% of bypass patients; hypoglycemia symptoms similar across groups
trialSummary.clinicalBottomLine: In obese patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes, bariatric surgery plus intensive medical therapy achieves superior glycemic control and medication reduction at 12 months vs medical therapy alone.

inclusionCriteria: Age 20–60 years; BMI 27–43; type 2 diabetes with HbA1c >7.0% on therapy or >6.5% if drug-naive
exclusionCriteria: Uncontrolled psychiatric disorder or substance abuse limiting adherence
keyTakeaways: Primary endpoint (HbA1c ≤6.0%): 12% medical · 42% bypass · 37% sleeve at 12 months.
Surgery: greater weight loss, fewer diabetes/CV meds; bypass > sleeve for some metabolic endpoints.
Nonblinded 12-month RCT; durability and long-term safety require extended follow-up.

items.title: STAMPEDE — Main Points (High-Yield Overview)
items.content:
Background and Objective: Few randomized trials had compared bariatric surgery with intensive medical therapy in moderately obese patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes. STAMPEDE evaluated intensive medical therapy alone versus medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy.

Study Design: 150 patients (BMI 27–43; mean HbA1c 9.2%) randomized to three groups. Primary endpoint: HbA1c ≤6.0% at 12 months.

Primary Outcomes: Primary endpoint achieved in 12% (medical) vs 42% (bypass) vs 37% (sleeve). Glycemic improvement was larger and more rapid with surgery.

Secondary Outcomes and Safety: Greater weight loss with surgery (-29.4 kg bypass, -25.1 kg sleeve vs -5.4 kg medical). Glucose-, lipid-, and BP-lowering medication use decreased after surgery and increased with medical therapy. No deaths or life-threatening complications; four reoperations.

Conclusions: Medical therapy plus bariatric surgery was significantly more effective than intensive medical therapy alone for glycemic control at 12 months.
keyPoints: HbA1c ≤6.0% at 12 mo: 12% medical · 42% bypass · 37% sleeve
Weight loss: -29.4 / -25.1 / -5.4 kg; meds ↓ with surgery
items.reference: Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Why was the STAMPEDE trial initiated?
items.answer: The trial was designed to address unanswered clinical questions regarding the relative efficacy of bariatric surgery compared to intensive medical therapy for improving glycemic control, specifically in moderately obese patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes.
keyPoints: The trial was designed to address unanswered clinical questions regarding the relative efficacy of bariatric surgery compared to intensive medical therapy for improving glycemic control, specifically in moderately obese patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q1: The trial was designed to address unanswered clinical questions regarding the re... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: How did the trial strictly define its primary clinical endpoint?
items.answer: The primary endpoint was defined as the proportion of patients who achieved a glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level of 6.0% or less—with or without the use of diabetes medications—exactly 12 months after randomization.
keyPoints: The primary endpoint was defined as the proportion of patients who achieved a glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level of 6.0% or less—with or without the use of diabetes medications—exactly 12 months after randomization.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q2: The primary endpoint was defined as the proportion of patients who achieved a gl... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Why did the researchers stratify patients according to their use of insulin at baseline?
items.answer: Stratification was used during the randomization process to ensure an equal distribution of patients requiring insulin across the three study groups, accounting for baseline disease severity.
keyPoints: Stratification was used during the randomization process to ensure an equal distribution of patients requiring insulin across the three study groups, accounting for baseline disease severity.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q3: Stratification was used during the randomization process to ensure an equal dist... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: How did the gastric bypass procedure structurally alter the patients' gastrointestinal anatomy?
items.answer: The Roux-en-Y gastric bypass involved creating a small 15-to-20-ml gastric pouch, a 150-cm Roux limb, and a 50-cm biliopancreatic limb.
keyPoints: The Roux-en-Y gastric bypass involved creating a small 15-to-20-ml gastric pouch, a 150-cm Roux limb, and a 50-cm biliopancreatic limb.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q4: The Roux-en-Y gastric bypass involved creating a small 15-to-20-ml gastric pouch... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Why did the average number of diabetes medications increase over 12 months for the medical-therapy group?
items.answer: Because the explicit goal of the intensive medical management was to modify and escalate diabetes medications until the patient successfully reached the therapeutic goal of an HbA1c of 6.0% or less.
keyPoints: Because the explicit goal of the intensive medical management was to modify and escalate diabetes medications until the patient successfully reached the therapeutic goal of an HbA1c of 6.0% or less.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q5: Because the explicit goal of the intensive medical management was to modify and ... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: How did weight loss outcomes compare between the three study groups at 12 months?
items.answer: Patients undergoing gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy experienced significantly greater mean weight loss (-29.4 kg and -25.1 kg, respectively) compared to those receiving medical therapy alone (-5.4 kg).
keyPoints: Patients undergoing gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy experienced significantly greater mean weight loss (-29.4 kg and -25.1 kg, respectively) compared to those receiving medical therapy alone (-5.4 kg).
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q6: Patients undergoing gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy experienced significan... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: How do the authors explain the physiological mechanism behind the improved glycemic control seen after surgery?
items.answer: The improvement appears to be driven by enhanced insulin sensitivity, marked by a reduction in fasting insulin levels and an improved HOMA-IR index, which may be linked to the attenuation of chronic systemic inflammation.
keyPoints: The improvement appears to be driven by enhanced insulin sensitivity, marked by a reduction in fasting insulin levels and an improved HOMA-IR index, which may be linked to the attenuation of chronic systemic inflammation.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q7: The improvement appears to be driven by enhanced insulin sensitivity, marked by ... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Why might the diabetes remission rates in this trial appear lower than those reported in earlier observational studies?
items.answer: The STAMPEDE trial enrolled patients with more advanced type 2 diabetes (average disease duration over 8 years, high baseline HbA1c, and high insulin use), whereas observational studies often used more liberal definitions of remission and lacked rigorous biochemical evidence.
keyPoints: The STAMPEDE trial enrolled patients with more advanced type 2 diabetes (average disease duration over 8 years, high baseline HbA1c, and high insulin use), whereas observational studies often used more liberal definitions of remission and lacked rigorous biochemical evidence.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q8: The STAMPEDE trial enrolled patients with more advanced type 2 diabetes (average... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: How did bariatric surgery impact the patients' reliance on cardiovascular medications?
items.answer: While lipid and blood-pressure levels were similar across all groups at 12 months, the surgical groups achieved these levels while requiring significantly fewer medications to treat hyperlipidemia and hypertension compared to the medical-therapy group.
keyPoints: While lipid and blood-pressure levels were similar across all groups at 12 months, the surgical groups achieved these levels while requiring significantly fewer medications to treat hyperlipidemia and hypertension compared to the medical-therapy group.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q9: While lipid and blood-pressure levels were similar across all groups at 12 month... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Why were some randomized patients excluded from the final 12-month data analysis?
items.answer: Eight patients withdrew from the study (seven in the medical-therapy group and one before undergoing sleeve gastrectomy), and an additional two patients in the medical-therapy group missed their 9-month and 12-month follow-up visits.
keyPoints: Eight patients withdrew from the study (seven in the medical-therapy group and one before undergoing sleeve gastrectomy), and an additional two patients in the medical-therapy group missed their 9-month and 12-month follow-up visits.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q10: Eight patients withdrew from the study (seven in the medical-therapy group and o... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: How did the surgical interventions alter markers of systemic inflammation compared to medical therapy?
items.answer: The surgical procedures resulted in a much more profound decrease in high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (CRP) levels—an 84% median reduction for gastric bypass and 80% for sleeve gastrectomy—compared to a 33.2% reduction for medical therapy alone.
keyPoints: The surgical procedures resulted in a much more profound decrease in high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (CRP) levels—an 84% median reduction for gastric bypass and 80% for sleeve gastrectomy—compared to a 33.2% reduction for medical therapy alone.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q11: The surgical procedures resulted in a much more profound decrease in high-sensit... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: How did medication reliance differ between patients who achieved the primary endpoint in the two surgical groups?
items.answer: Every patient in the gastric-bypass group who achieved an HbA1c of 6.0% or less did so completely without diabetes medications, whereas 28% of the patients in the sleeve-gastrectomy group who reached the target still required one or more glucose-lowering drugs.
keyPoints: Every patient in the gastric-bypass group who achieved an HbA1c of 6.0% or less did so completely without diabetes medications, whereas 28% of the patients in the sleeve-gastrectomy group who reached the target still required one or more glucose-lowering drugs.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q12: Every patient in the gastric-bypass group who achieved an HbA1c of 6.0% or less ... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Why do the authors suggest that weight loss is not the exclusive driver of diabetes resolution post-surgery?
items.answer: They note that significant reductions in the use of diabetes medications occurred early on, before patients had achieved their maximal weight loss, suggesting that alterations in gut hormones immediately impact glycemic physiology.
keyPoints: They note that significant reductions in the use of diabetes medications occurred early on, before patients had achieved their maximal weight loss, suggesting that alterations in gut hormones immediately impact glycemic physiology.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q13: They note that significant reductions in the use of diabetes medications occurre... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: How was the sleeve gastrectomy surgically executed in this trial?
items.answer: The procedure reduced the gastric volume by 75 to 80% by resecting the stomach alongside a 30-French endoscope, beginning 3 cm from the pylorus and ending at the angle of His.
keyPoints: The procedure reduced the gastric volume by 75 to 80% by resecting the stomach alongside a 30-French endoscope, beginning 3 cm from the pylorus and ending at the angle of His.
items.reference: STAMPEDE Q14: The procedure reduced the gastric volume by 75 to 80% by resecting the stomach a... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Why do the authors classify the durability and long-term safety profile of these results as uncertain?
items.answer: Because the study only reports on a relatively short 12-month follow-up period, making a planned 4-year extension study necessary to properly evaluate long-term outcomes and potential late surgical complications.
keyPoints: Because the study only reports on a relatively short 12-month follow-up period, making a planned 4-year extension study necessary to properly evaluate long-term outcomes and potential late surgical complications.

items.question: What was the explicit target HbA1c level defining the primary endpoint?
items.explanation: Primary endpoint was HbA1c ≤6.0% at 12 months.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 1: Primary endpoint was HbA1c ≤6.0% at 12 months. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: What percentage of patients in the gastric-bypass group successfully achieved the primary endpoint at 12 months?
items.explanation: 42% of gastric-bypass patients achieved HbA1c ≤6.0%.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 2: 42% of gastric-bypass patients achieved HbA1c ≤6.0%. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Which treatment group experienced the greatest absolute reduction in Body Mass Index (BMI) at 12 months?
options: Intensive medical therapy
Roux-en-Y gastric bypass
Sleeve gastrectomy
Both surgical groups had identical reductions
items.explanation: Gastric bypass produced the greatest BMI reduction.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 3: Gastric bypass produced the greatest BMI reduction. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: What was the mean baseline duration of diabetes for patients randomized into the medical-therapy group?
options: 2.5 years
5.5 years
8.9 years
12.0 years
items.explanation: Mean diabetes duration was 8.9 years in the medical-therapy group.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 4: Mean diabetes duration was 8.9 years in the medical-therapy group. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Across the surgical groups, how many patients required a reoperation to manage complications?
items.explanation: Four surgical patients required reoperation.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 5: Four surgical patients required reoperation. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Which vitamin or mineral supplement was explicitly included in the gastric-bypass protocol but NOT in the sleeve-gastrectomy protocol?
options: Multivitamin
Vitamin B12
Iron and calcium citrate with vitamin D
Folic acid
items.explanation: Iron and calcium citrate with vitamin D were required for bypass but not sleeve.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 6: Iron and calcium citrate with vitamin D were required for bypass but not sleeve. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.explanation: 44% of patients were on insulin at baseline.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 7: 44% of patients were on insulin at baseline. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Which of the following was a strict exclusion criterion for participating in the STAMPEDE trial?
options: A BMI greater than 35
Previous bariatric surgery
An age over 50 years
A history of clinical dyslipidemia
items.explanation: Previous bariatric surgery was an exclusion criterion.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 8: Previous bariatric surgery was an exclusion criterion. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.explanation: Medical-therapy group mean weight loss was -5.4 kg.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 9: Medical-therapy group mean weight loss was -5.4 kg. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: By month 12, what percentage of patients in the medical-therapy group still required the use of insulin?
items.explanation: 38% of medical-therapy patients still required insulin at 12 months.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 10: 38% of medical-therapy patients still required insulin at 12 months. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.question: Which surgical procedure demonstrated a statistically significant decrease in triglycerides compared to medical therapy at 12 months?
options: Sleeve gastrectomy only
Gastric bypass only
Both surgical procedures
Neither procedure
items.explanation: Only gastric bypass showed significant triglyceride reduction vs medical therapy.
items.reference: STAMPEDE MCQ 11: Only gastric bypass showed significant triglyceride reduction vs medical therapy. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

≤ 150 mg/dL

items.question: How did high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol change from baseline in the gastric-bypass group?
options: It decreased by 28.5%
It increased by 11.3%
It increased by 28.5%
It remained completely unchanged
items.explanation: HDL increased 28.5% from baseline in the gastric-bypass group.

options: Gastrointestinal leak requiring intravenous treatment for dehydration
Hospitalization
Hemoglobin decrease ≥ 5 g/dL
Cholelithiasis

items.question: At baseline, what proportion of the enrolled participants were women?
items.explanation: 66% of participants were women.

It gradually increased

items.explanation: Diabetes medication count decreased significantly in surgical groups.

options: ≤ 110 mm Hg
≤ 120 mm Hg
≤ 130 mm Hg
≤ 140 mm Hg

items.question: How many patients were required in each group to provide 80% power to detect a primary outcome difference between the two surgical groups?
items.explanation: 50 patients per group (150 total) powered surgical-group comparisons.

items.statement: The study reported a highly significant difference in the achievement of the primary endpoint between the gastric-bypass and sleeve-gastrectomy surgical groups.
items.explanation: No significant difference between bypass and sleeve for primary endpoint.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 1: No significant difference between bypass and sleeve for primary endpoint. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: Intraoperative biopsies revealed that more than 60% of the surgical patients had moderate-to-severe fatty liver disease.
items.explanation: >60% of surgical patients had moderate-to-severe fatty liver on biopsy.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 2: >60% of surgical patients had moderate-to-severe fatty liver on biopsy. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: The intensive medical-therapy group failed to achieve any reduction in body weight over the 12-month observation period.
items.explanation: Medical-therapy group lost a mean of 5.4 kg.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 3: Medical-therapy group lost a mean of 5.4 kg. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: Self-reported symptoms of hypoglycemia occurred at a similar frequency in both the surgical and medical groups.
items.explanation: Hypoglycemia symptoms were similarly frequent across groups.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 4: Hypoglycemia symptoms were similarly frequent across groups. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: The STAMPEDE trial was conducted using a strictly double-blinded methodology to prevent patient bias.
items.explanation: Trial was randomized and nonblinded (open-label surgery).
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 5: Trial was randomized and nonblinded (open-label surgery). — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: By the 12-month mark, the number of patients requiring three or more diabetes medications fell to zero in the gastric-bypass group.
items.explanation: No bypass patients required ≥3 diabetes medications at 12 months.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 6: No bypass patients required ≥3 diabetes medications at 12 months. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: There were zero deaths and zero life-threatening complications among the 150 randomized patients during the 12-month trial.
items.explanation: No deaths or life-threatening complications during 12-month trial.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 7: No deaths or life-threatening complications during 12-month trial. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: At baseline, the average HbA1c level across all enrolled patients was exactly 7.0%.
items.explanation: Mean baseline HbA1c was 9.2%, not 7.0%.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 8: Mean baseline HbA1c was 9.2%, not 7.0%. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: The HOMA-IR index, an indicator of insulin resistance, improved significantly more in the medical-therapy group than in the surgical groups.
items.explanation: HOMA-IR improved more in surgical groups than medical therapy.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 9: HOMA-IR improved more in surgical groups than medical therapy. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: As part of their intensive lifestyle counseling, patients in the trial were actively encouraged to participate in the Weight Watchers program.
items.explanation: Patients were encouraged to participate in Weight Watchers.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 10: Patients were encouraged to participate in Weight Watchers. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: To isolate the effects of the surgery, the trial strictly excluded any patients who were using insulin therapy prior to randomization.
items.explanation: Insulin users were stratified, not excluded; 44% used insulin at baseline.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 11: Insulin users were stratified, not excluded; 44% used insulin at baseline. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: The proportion of patients requiring lipid-lowering drugs in the gastric-bypass group dropped from 86% at baseline to just 27% at 12 months.
items.explanation: Lipid-lowering drug use fell from 86% to 27% in bypass group.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 12: Lipid-lowering drug use fell from 86% to 27% in bypass group. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: The results demonstrated that intensive medical therapy alone is capable of producing a substantial and significant reduction in HbA1c levels.
items.explanation: Medical therapy produced significant HbA1c reduction, though less than surgery.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 13: Medical therapy produced significant HbA1c reduction, though less than surgery. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: A reoperation procedure was required for one patient to treat a gastrointestinal leak following their sleeve gastrectomy.
items.explanation: One sleeve patient required reoperation for GI leak.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 14: One sleeve patient required reoperation for GI leak. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.statement: Patients randomized into the surgical groups did not receive the intensive medical therapy protocol utilized by the control group.
items.explanation: All groups received intensive medical therapy; surgery was added to medical therapy.
items.reference: STAMPEDE T/F 15: All groups received intensive medical therapy; surgery was added to medical therapy. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabetes. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1567-1576.

items.stem: What was the primary objective of the STAMPEDE trial?
options: A) To compare the efficacy of bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for glycemic control in moderately obese patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes
B) To evaluate long-term weight loss outcomes of gastric bypass versus sleeve gastrectomy in severely obese patients
C) To assess the role of pharmacologic therapy in conjunction with lifestyle modification for diabetes management
D) To determine the safety profile of bariatric surgery in patients with a BMI <30
items.explanation: The STAMPEDE trial specifically aimed to compare bariatric surgery (sleeve gastrectomy) with intensive medical therapy for glycemic control in moderately obese patients (BMI 30-35) with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes.

items.stem: How was the primary clinical endpoint defined in the trial?
options: A) The proportion of patients achieving an HbA1c ≤6.0% at 12 months, regardless of diabetes medication use
B) The proportion of patients achieving an HbA1c ≤7.0% at 6 months, regardless of diabetes medication use
C) The proportion of patients achieving an HbA1c ≤5.5% at 24 months, regardless of diabetes medication use
D) The proportion of patients achieving an HbA1c ≤6.0% at 12 months, only while using diabetes medications
items.explanation: The primary endpoint was specifically defined as HbA1c ≤6.0% at 12 months, with or without diabetes medications, as stated in the key point.

items.stem: Why did the researchers stratify patients according to their use of insulin at baseline?
options: A: Stratification was used during the randomization process to ensure an equal distribution of patients requiring insulin across the three study groups, accounting for baseline disease severity.
B: Stratification was used to ensure similar age distribution across groups and reduce confounding by age.
C: Stratification was used to minimize variability in treatment adherence across study groups.
D: Stratification was used to ensure all groups had comparable baseline HbA1c levels.
items.explanation: Stratification by insulin use ensures balanced group allocation for a key prognostic factor (insulin dependence), which reflects baseline disease severity and improves study validity.

items.stem: Which of the following accurately describes the structural alterations in the gastrointestinal anatomy after a Roux-en-Y gastric bypass?
options: A. Creation of a 15-to-20-ml gastric pouch, a 150-cm Roux limb, and a 50-cm biliopancreatic limb
B. Creation of a 50-to-100-ml gastric pouch, a 100-cm Roux limb, and a 100-cm biliopancreatic limb
C. Creation of a 15-to-20-ml gastric pouch, a 50-cm Roux limb, and a 150-cm biliopancreatic limb
D. Creation of a 100-to-150-ml gastric pouch, a 200-cm Roux limb, and no biliopancreatic limb
items.explanation: The Roux-en-Y gastric bypass typically involves a small gastric pouch (15–20 mL), a longer Roux limb (150 cm), and a shorter biliopancreatic limb (50 cm) to achieve malabsorption and restriction.

items.explanation: Surgical interventions (gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy) produced significantly greater weight loss than medical therapy alone, as evidenced by the mean values reported.

items.stem: What is the primary physiological mechanism proposed for improved glycemic control after surgery?
options: A) Enhanced insulin sensitivity with reduced fasting insulin levels and improved HOMA-IR, linked to reduced systemic inflammation
B) Increased insulin secretion from preserved pancreatic beta-cell function
C) Weight loss due to caloric restriction during postoperative recovery
D) Altered gut microbiota composition promoting glucose metabolism
items.explanation: The improvement is attributed to enhanced insulin sensitivity, reduced fasting insulin, and improved HOMA-IR, which correlate with decreased chronic inflammation rather than direct pancreatic or microbiota effects.

items.stem: Why might diabetes remission rates in the STAMPEDE trial appear lower compared to earlier observational studies?
options: A) The trial enrolled patients with more advanced type 2 diabetes and used stricter biochemical criteria for remission
B) The trial used a shorter follow-up period, whereas observational studies tracked outcomes over decades
C) Observational studies excluded patients on insulin, leading to artificially higher remission rates
D) The trial enrolled a younger population with less comorbidities, making remission less likely
items.explanation: The STAMPEDE trial's stricter biochemical remission criteria and enrollment of patients with more advanced disease (longer duration, higher HbA1c, higher insulin use) likely contributed to lower observed remission rates compared to observational studies with more liberal definitions.

C. Both surgical procedures result in a 50% reduction, with medical therapy showing no significant change
D. Surgical interventions increase CRP levels, while medical therapy reduces them by 40%
items.explanation: Surgical procedures (gastric bypass and sleeve gastrectomy) produce significantly greater reductions in CRP levels (84% and 80%, respectively) compared to medical therapy (33.2% reduction), as demonstrated in clinical studies.

B) Weight loss directly improves insulin sensitivity independent of gut hormones
C) Post-surgical dietary restrictions alone account for diabetes resolution
D) Bariatric surgery reduces caloric intake, which is the primary mechanism for diabetes remission
items.explanation: Early medication reductions before maximal weight loss suggest that gut hormone changes, rather than weight loss alone, drive early glycemic improvements.

items.stem: How was the sleeve gastrectomy surgically executed in this trial?
options: A: The procedure reduced the gastric volume by 75 to 80% by resecting the stomach alongside a 30-French endoscope, beginning 3 cm from the pylorus and ending at the angle of His.
B: The procedure reduced the gastric volume by 50 to 60% using a 40-French endoscope, starting at the fundus and ending at the cardia.
C: The procedure involved removing 25 to 30% of the stomach with a 24-French endoscope, beginning 5 cm from the pylorus and ending at the lesser curvature.
D: The procedure utilized a 36-French endoscope to resect 85 to 90% of the stomach, starting at the cardia and ending at the angle of His.
items.explanation: The correct technique involves a 30-French endoscope, 75-80% volume reduction, and resection from 3 cm proximal to the pylorus to the angle of His, as described in the key points.

items.assertion: The durability and long-term safety profile of these results is classified as uncertain.
items.reason: Because the study only reports on a relatively short 12-month follow-up period, making a planned 4-year extension study necessary to properly evaluate long-term outcomes and potential late surgical complications.
items.explanation: The short follow-up period limits assessment of long-term outcomes and late complications, justifying the classification of durability and safety as uncertain.
